goal_16.config.title: Inequalities in AI use and exposure
goal_16.config.abstract: AI is reshaping the world, but the digital shift is filtering slowly to the global poor. While wealthy nations race ahead, high costs and a lack of digital skills leave billions behind. In poorer countries, the workplace remains mostly untouched by AI, threatening to turn a technological leap into a permanent global divide
goal_16.config.citation: Mahler, Daniel Gerszon, Jan Willem Tulp, He Wang, and Michael Weber. 2026. “Inequalities in AI use and exposure” In Atlas of  Global Development 2026,  edited by A. F. Pirlea, D. Wadhwa, D. Mahler, U. Serajuddin, A. Thudt, and M. Welch. Washington, DC: World Bank. License: Creative Commons Attribution CC BY 3.0 IGO. https://www.doi.org/10.60616/pp3f-d004
goal_16.config.credits: Daniel Gerszon Mahler, He Wang, Michael Weber
goal_16.config.inputs: David Castillo Parra, Gabriel Demombynes, Holly Krambeck, Jorg Gero Langbein, Yan Liu, Sharada Srinivasan
goal_16.config.homeFactoidText: of people in low-income countries have the digital skills to use AI
goal_16.config.homeTagline: While high-income nations rapidly integrate AI, stark infrastructural and skills gaps are leaving low-income countries behind
goal_16.content.#1.text: Artificial intelligence (AI) is a rapidly spreading transformative technology. AI can be defined as a branch of computer science dedicated to creating systems capable of performing tasks that typically require human intelligence.[reference: worldbank2025] It has the potential to fundamentally shift how we learn, work, and live our daily lives. The unprecedented pace of AI adoption holds immense potential for accelerating global development and improving living standards. For example, it offers the potential of boosting growth,[reference: bergman2025] personalizing education,[reference: molina2024] and transforming the agricultural sector.[reference: krishnakumari2025] However, the spread of AI is far from even.
goal_16.content.#2.text: Measuring the use of AI is challenging, in part due to the many ways one can interact with AI and the speed with which this is changing. High-frequency website traffic data reveals a great divergence in the use of AI across countries. This is in part due to greater internet penetration in wealthier nations, but AI use per internet user is also much greater in wealthier nations. Focusing on the most used AI platform, ChatGPT, as of May 2025, visits per internet user in high-income countries was nearly 50 times that of low-income countries, 8 times that of lower-middle income countries, and 3 times that of upper-middle income countries.[reference: liu2025][reference: semrush2025]
goal_16.content.#3.text: Visits to ChatGPT per internet user is [emphasis: 50 times] greater in high-income countries than in low-income countries, as of May 2025.[reference: liu2025][reference: semrush2025]
goal_16.content.#4.title: Internet users in rich countries use ChatGPT 50 times more than in poorer countries
goal_16.content.#4.subtitle: Visits to ChatGPT per internet user, May 2025
goal_16.content.#4.source: Liu, Huang, and Wang (2025), sourced from Semrush.
goal_16.content.#4.visdescription: A global map of traffic to chatGPT per internet user and a scatter plot showing a positive correlation between GDP per capita and chatGPT usage
goal_16.content.#4.prompt_text: Where in the world are people using AI?
goal_16.content.#4.keyFactLabel: more ChatGPT usage occurs per internet user in high-income countries than in low-income countries.
goal_16.content.#5.text: This discrepancy is in part due to the use of different AI platforms around the world. For example, OpenAI, the company behind ChatGPT, is less present in China where other companies dominate. Measures that account for multiple platforms likewise find that in most countries in Sub-Saharan Africa, less than 10 percent of the working-age population is using AI.[reference: misra2025] Evidence from Anthropic, the company behind the AI assistant, Claude, also suggests that country-usage is lowest in poorer countries, which have usage scores 10 times smaller than high-income countries.[reference: anthropic2026]
goal_16.content.#7.text: The disparity in AI adoption is not accidental, it is rooted in difficult development challenges. The Digital Progress and Trends Report 2025 finds that to fully benefit from AI, countries need (1) reliable and affordable internet connectivity, (2) local data that makes AI tools useful and accurate, (3) a digitally skilled population, and (4) enough computing power. Without these elements in place, the promise of AI could remain out of reach for a significant portion of the world’s population.
goal_16.content.#8.text: [emphasis: 1. Connectivity and affordability: ] A primary barrier to AI adoption in low- and middle-income countries is inadequate internet infrastructure. [reference: pirlea2026] The absence of fiber networks for many rural and low-income populations means that they have to rely on mobile devices. Among those with internet access over phones, usage remains low. This is partly because data packages are prohibitively costly for many in poorer countries. In low-income countries, the price of a data-only 5GB mobile package makes up more than 15 percent of the average person's total budget. In these countries, around 60 percent of the household budget is allocated to food.[reference: mahler2022] This means that a data package would require nearly half of the budget left after satisfying nutritional needs.
goal_16.content.#10.text: In low-income countries, a data-only 5GB mobile package would take up[emphasis:  nearly half] of the household budget that remains after food consumption.
goal_16.content.#11.text: As a result, many households in low‑income countries face a trade‑off between affording a 5 GB data package for AI and spending on essentials such as health care, education, or clothing.
goal_16.content.#13.title: Mobile data is prohibitively expensive in low-income countries
goal_16.content.#13.subtitle: Cost of data-only 5GB mobile package as a share of household budget
goal_16.content.#13.source: International Telecommunication Union (ITU), World Bank’s Poverty and Inequality Platform (PIP), Mahler et al. (2022), and U.S. Department of Agriculture Economic Research Service.
goal_16.content.#13.visdescription: Shows the cost of a data package relative to a household’s budget, by income group, and relative to food expenditure.
goal_16.content.#13.prompt_text: Can everyone afford to use AI?
goal_16.content.#13.keyFactLabel: of the budget remaining after food consumption in low-income countries would be taken up by a data-only 5GB mobile package.
goal_16.content.#13.legendLabelRemaining: Household budget that remains after food consumption
goal_16.content.#16.text: The relatively high cost of mobile phone data and the difficulty of creating fiber networks throughout low- and middle-income countries underscores the importance of 'Small AI' – lightweight applications that are optimized for mobile-first environments, and can often function with limited or intermittent connectivity. For example, agricultural AI tools that identify crop diseases from a photo can perform their core analysis on-device, minimizing data usage and making them viable for farmers in remote areas.[reference: kim2025]
goal_16.content.#17.text: [emphasis: 2. The Digital Language Divide.] Large language models, AI systems built on vast datasets to predict and generate human-like text, are only effective when they understand a language’s well. However, the digital world is dominated by English, which accounts for nearly half of global URLs and a disproportionate share of the high-quality text data used to build leading AI models. While substantial datasets exist for other languages spoken in high-income countries, there is a significant deficit for those primarily spoken in low and middle-income countries. Consequently, AI models often lack the accuracy and nuance needed to be useful in these contexts. [reference: solatorio2024] This limits the utility of current AI tools while perpetuating a digital world where only a fraction of global cultures and languages are well represented. If AI is to be a universal tool, it must learn to navigate the world’s full linguistic map. [reference: unesco2025]
goal_16.content.#18.title: Languages spoken in poorer countries are underrepresented in AI training data
goal_16.content.#18.subtitle: Global URLs and global population, by language
goal_16.content.#18.source: Common Crawl, LinguaMeta, and Solatorio et al. (2024) (based on data from Etnologue).
goal_16.content.#18.visdescription: Compares how present languages are online with how much they are spoken. Shows that languages spoken in poorer countries tend to be underrepresented online
goal_16.content.#18.prompt_text: Are AI tools equally useful and accurate in all languages?
goal_16.content.#18.xAxisLabel: Share of global population speaking language (log)
goal_16.content.#22.text: [emphasis: 3. Competency: The Human Capital Gap. ]A third barrier is the global deficit in digital skills. Using AI effectively requires digital literacy and for certain applications advanced technical skills. In many low- and middle-income countries, these skills are in short supply. In 2023, it was estimated that less than five percent of the population in low-income countries possessed basic digital skills, defined as basic use of digital devices such as email communication, web search and online transactions.
goal_16.content.#23.label: in low-income countries had basic digital skills in 2023.[reference: ITU]
goal_16.content.#24.text: The gap is even more severe for advanced skills, which relate to data analysis, software development and high-level computing competences with AI systems. Without a concerted effort to build digital skills at all levels of the education system and to retain local talent, poorer countries risk being unable to move from passive consumers of AI to active participants and creators in the AI economy.
goal_16.content.#25.title: Less than 5% in low-income countries have the digital skills to use AI
goal_16.content.#25.subtitle: Population with digital skills, 2023
goal_16.content.#25.source: World Bank (2025), based on data from the International Telecommunication Union (ITU). Note: Basic digital skills refer to basic use of digital devices, email communication, web search and online transactions. Intermediate skills refer to use of software and data management. Advanced skills refer to data analysis, software development and high-level computing competences.
goal_16.content.#25.visdescription: Shows the share of the population with basic, intermediate, and advanced digital skills, by income group
goal_16.content.#25.prompt_text: Does everyone have the skills to use AI?
goal_16.content.#25.basicSkillsTooltipTe: Basic digital skills refer to basic use of digital devices, email communication, web search and online transactions.
goal_16.content.#25.intermediateSkillsTo: Intermediate skills refer to use of software and data management.
goal_16.content.#25.advancedSkillsToolti: Advanced skills refer to data analysis, software development and high-level computing competences.
goal_16.content.#27.text: [emphasis: 4. Computing power.] A fourth barrier is the lack of AI chips, memory chips, and storage chips to build servers, which constrains computing power in many poorer countries. As of June 2025, high-income countries accounted for 77 percent of global data center capacity, middle income countries for 23 percent, and low-income countries less than 0.1 percent.[reference: worldbank2025] On a per capita basis, the US has 20,000 times as many servers as low-income countries and 200 times as many as middle-income countries.[reference: worldbank2025]. High entry costs, lack of energy, and digital infrastructure constraints often deter private investment in poorer countries.
goal_16.content.#28.text: Fortunately, computing power is highly tradable across countries. With access from other nations, poor countries can use AI even without having national computing power.
goal_16.content.#30.text: There is a direct link between this lack of usage of AI in low- and middle-income countries and lack of exposure to AI at work.[reference: dem2026] This matters, because exposure to AI means that some tasks can be augmented by AI, which can increase workers’ productivity, while others may be automated, reducing demand for some jobs. Whichever it is, high exposure suggests that the nature of these jobs is likely to change.
goal_16.content.#32.text: One approach to measuring workers’ exposure is the AI Occupational Exposure index, which links human job tasks to various artificial intelligence capabilities.[reference: felten2021] It uses a detailed database of occupations to determine how much of a person's daily work duties overlap with current AI capabilities. By analyzing these connections, the index identifies which jobs are most likely to be affected or assisted by the emerging technology. The scale ranges from 0 to 100, where 0 represents an occupation with no tasks suitable for AI and 100 indicates a role where every task is fully exposed to AI capabilities. Though the capabilities of AI have expanded significantly since this index was constructed, recent usage data is broadly consistent with the index, showing that AI use is closely aligned with the occupations found most exposed to AI according to the index.[reference: dem2026][footnote: Yet the link between use and exposure is not perfect. Managers, for example, show high theoretical exposure to AI but relatively low usage.]
goal_16.content.#36.title: Workers in low-income countries are less exposed to AI
goal_16.content.#36.subtitle: Index of occupational exposure to AI from 0 (no exposure) to 100 (full exposure)
goal_16.content.#36.source: Author's calculations based on Demombynes, Langbein, and Weber (2025). Note: The occupational icons are AI generated.
goal_16.content.#36.visdescription: Shows the occupational exposure to AI by occupation type and income group
goal_16.content.#36.keyFactLabel: of workers in low-income countries are highly exposed to AI.
goal_16.content.#36.scenes.#0.text: Some occupations, such as Information and Communications Technology Professionals, are exposed to AI in most facets of their work, with an exposure score of 93.
goal_16.content.#36.scenes.#1.text: Others, such as Subsistence Farmers, Fishers, Hunters and Gatherers, are largely not exposed by AI in their work, with an exposure score of 20.[footnote: The tasks associated with an occupation differ in rich and poor countries. The AI Occupational Exposure Index is based on the tasks associated with occupations in the US. In poorer countries, similar occupations likely have tasks with less AI exposure.]
goal_16.content.#36.scenes.#2.text: Most occupations fall somewhere in between.
goal_16.content.#36.scenes.#3.text: Countries are exposed differently to AI because occupation types differ by country. We can see this by scaling the occupations according to how prominent they are. Here this is done for the United States. Defining high exposure as greater than 50, about half of all workers in the US are highly exposed, because occupations such as Business and Administration Associate Professionals and Teaching Professionals are common.
goal_16.content.#36.scenes.#4.text: In low-income countries, to the contrary, where large populations of workers are engaged in farming, less than 20 percent of workers are highly exposed to AI.[footnote: These statistics depend on where one sets the threshold for high exposure. Regardless of how it is defined, workers in low-income countries are notably less exposed to AI than workers in high-income countries.]
goal_16.content.#37.text: The difference between exposure in poor and rich countries is likely underestimated for two reasons: Firstly, many in poorer countries lack internet and computers at home or at work. Therefore, even if their occupational tasks could be exposed to AI, in practice, they are not.[reference: gmyrek2024] Secondly, the tasks associated with an occupation differ in rich and poor countries. The AI Occupational Exposure Index is based on the tasks associated with occupations in the US. In poorer countries, similar occupations likely have tasks with less AI exposure.
goal_16.content.#38.text: AI exposure also varies notably within countries. Exposure is heavily concentrated among urban populations, with city dwellers facing much higher exposure than those in rural areas due to the nature of urban employment. Education is the strongest predictor of exposure, as individuals with post-secondary degrees occupy roles with high cognitive task intensity that are most susceptible to AI integration. A notable gender gap exists, with women generally experiencing higher exposure than men, largely because they are more concentrated in clerical, professional, and service roles.[reference: dem2025][reference: wadhwa2026] Understanding these differences in exposure is vital to anticipate how AI will transform the labor market, and the socio-economic consequences of this transformation.
goal_16.content.#39.text: In middle-income countries, usage is highly concentrated among a few professions; ICT workers and teachers account for nearly three-quarters of all AI usage, compared to a much broader spread across sectors in high-income countries. This concentration reinforces the impact of foundational barriers, as adoption remains restricted to workers with the strongest digital access and text-heavy tasks.[reference: dem2026]
goal_16.content.#41.text: The rise of AI offers a powerful engine for productivity and human development, yet it also threatens to deepen existing inequalities between and within countries. The impact of AI on jobs remains a subject of intense debate, with the future likely to involve a complex mix of both augmentation, where AI enhances human capabilities, and automation, where AI replaces certain tasks. Until now, the direct impact in the poorest countries has been limited by structural barriers and the predominant occupations workers have. As AI adoption follows a predictable diffusion pattern—starting with ICT professionals and spreading outward to other sectors—it offers a window for proactive policy.
goal_16.content.#42.text: For low- and middle-income countries, the path forward involves a dual strategy: First, strengthening foundational pillars by investing in the essentials such as affordable internet, data infrastructure, and digital skills.
goal_16.content.#43.text: Second, building the bedrock for 'Small AI', which does not require massive infrastructure or cutting-edge servers. Instead, it flourishes on smaller, local datasets and is fine-tuned to address immediate challenges. By focusing on solutions that can run on everyday smartphones using minimal resources, poorer countries can harness AI to create tangible impact in key sectors like public health, agriculture, and education.
